ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301492 covers plain yogurt; Thairu is a South Indian set-curd yogurt made by fermenting full-fat cow or buffalo milk with live cultures, which is the closest defensible FoodOn match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Yoghurt, plain) is the closest defensible match for Thairu, a plain fermented milk curd without added flavours or sugar.

main_ingredients: live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Thairu is approximately 150 g (one small bowl), commonly consumed as an accompaniment to rice meals or as a standalone snack.

nutrient_source.title: Thairu (Curd / Plain Yogurt) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are preserved as supplied. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 36 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain whole-milk yogurt (unsweetened), which is the closest approved analogous food to Thairu. Thairu is a plain fermented set curd made from whole milk with live cultures, functionally identical to plain whole-milk yogurt in composition and fermentation profile. GL per serving = (36 × 5.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.8, rounded to 3.5 using 150 g serving and carbohydrate of 5.2 g/100g: (36 × 7.8) / 100 = 2.8 ≈ 2.8; reported as 2.8 rounded to one decimal.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (123 mg/100g) and calcium (152 mg/100g) supporting vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Thairu is suitable for hypertensive individuals; its sodium content of 123 mg/100g is moderate and its calcium at 152 mg/100g supports vascular tone — consume plain and unsweetened, avoiding flavoured or salted commercial variants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (36), naturally occurring lactose sugar (3.7 g/100g), and protein (4 g/100g) slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thairu has a low glycemic index of approximately 36 and contains only naturally occurring lactose; it is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed plain and unsweetened, consistent with ADA dietary guidelines for fermented dairy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.8 g/100g), probiotic fermentation, and calcium (152 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain Thairu provides probiotic cultures that support gut-cardiovascular axis health; saturated fat at approximately 2.8 g/100g is within acceptable limits for moderate daily consumption, and individuals with CVD should choose low-fat variants if consuming large portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic fermentation (Lactobacillus, Streptococcus) and calcium (152 mg/100g) supporting bone protection
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thairu qualifies as a probiotic fermented food that supports gut microbiome and immune regulation; calcium at 152 mg/100g is beneficial for bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids — avoid sweetened variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thairu (also spelled Thayir) is a traditional South Indian fermented milk curd, closely equivalent to plain whole-milk yogurt. It is made by inoculating warm full-fat cow or buffalo milk with live bacterial cultures — primarily Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus — and allowing it to set at room temperature for 6–8 hours. The result is a thick, creamy, mildly tangy curd with a smooth texture. Thairu is a staple in South Indian cuisine, consumed daily as an accompaniment to rice (curd rice or thayir sadam), as a cooling side dish with spicy curries, or as a standalone snack. It is also the base for raita, lassi, and buttermilk (moru). Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 77 kcal, 4 g protein, 4.5 g fat (of which approximately 2.8 g is saturated), 5.2 g carbohydrate (3.7 g naturally occurring lactose sugar), 0.7 g dietary fibre, 123 mg sodium, and 152 mg calcium. Its glycemic index is approximately 36 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The probiotic cultures support gut health and immune regulation, and the calcium content supports bone health. Thairu is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal. It is widely consumed across Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, and Karnataka, and is also popular in the South Indian diaspora communities of Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is Thairu the same as yogurt?
What is the GI of Thairu?
Can I eat Thairu if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171284, plain whole-milk yogurt) as the closest approved analogous source. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 tables.
notes: Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (1.0 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.1 g/100g), trans fat (0.0 g/100g), cholesterol (13 mg/100g), potassium (180 mg/100g), and micronutrient values (vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284) as HPB does not separately report these fields for Thairu. All core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) are non-zero and sourced from HPB at confidence 0.95. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain whole-milk yogurt, which is functionally equivalent to Thairu. GL per 150 g serving = (36 × 5.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.8 g. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained: all fields are populated with either HPB values, USDA cross-reference estimates, or confirmed trace amounts. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100g as plain fermented whole-milk yogurt contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; trace ruminant trans fats (CLA) are below reporting threshold.
